outlook.e: Perched on the edge of the cliff was a hidden outlook.

outrage.d: To cause or commit an outrage upon; to treat with violence or abuse.
outrage.e: The decision caused widespread outrage among the public.

outsider.d: One who is not part of a community or organization.
outsider.e: While the initiated easily understand the symbols, they are wholly inaccessible to outsiders.

overlook.d: To offer a view (of something) from a higher position.
overlook.e: Our hotel room overlooks the lake.

overly.e: Parents can be overly protective of their children.

oversee.d: To supervise, guide, review or direct the actions of a person or group.
oversee.e: It is congress's duty to oversee the spending of federal funds.

overturn.d: To turn over, capsize or upset.
overturn.e: The high court overturned the lower court's ruling.

overwhelm.d: To engulf, surge over and submerge.
overwhelm.e: The dinghy was overwhelmed by the great wave.

pad.d: A flattened mass of anything soft, to sit or lie on.

parameter.d: An input variable of a procedure definition, that gets an actual value (argument) at execution time (formal parameter).
parameter.e: Roughly, a tuple of arguments could be thought of as a vector, whereas a tuple of parameters could be thought of as a covector (i.e., linear functional). When a function is called, a parameter tuple becomes "bound" to an argument tuple, allowing the function instance itself to be computed to yield a return value. This would be roughly analogous to applying a covector to a vector (by taking their dot product (or, rather, matrix-product of row vector and column vector)) to obtain a scalar.

parental.d: Of or relating to a parent

parish.d: In the Anglican, Eastern Orthodox, Lutheran and Roman Catholic Church, an administrative part of a diocese that has its own church.

parliamentary.d: Of, relating to, or enacted by a parliament

partial.d: Describing a property that holds only when an algorithm terminates
partial.e: It's easy to prove partial correctness, but it's not obvious that it is also totally correct.

partially.d: To a partial degree or extent, incompletely.
partially.e: The road was partially blocked by the fallen tree.

passing.d: Death, dying; the end of something.

passive.d: Being subjected to an action without producing a reaction.
passive.e: He remained passive during the protest.

pastor.d: A shepherd; someone who tends to a flock of animals.

patch.d: A cover worn over a damaged eye, an eyepatch.
patch.e: He had scratched his cornea so badly that his doctor told him to wear a patch.

patent.d: A declaration issued by a government agency declaring someone the inventor of a new invention and having the privilege of stopping others from making, using or selling the claimed invention; a letter patent.

pathway.d: A footpath or other path or track.
pathway.e: The pathway through the garden was lined with flowers.

patrol.d: To go the rounds of, as a sentry, guard, or policeman

patron.d: One who protects or supports; a defender or advocate.
patron.e: This car park is for patrons only.

peak.d: A point; the sharp end or top of anything that terminates in a point; as, the peak, or front, of a cap.
peak.e: The stock market reached a peak in September 1929.

peasant.d: An uncouth, crude or ill-bred person.
peasant.e: The peasant farmers worked hard to cultivate the land.

peculiar.d: Out of the ordinary; odd; strange; unusual.
peculiar.e: It would be rather peculiar to see a kangaroo hopping down a city street.

persist.d: To cause to persist; make permanent.
persist.e: If the symptoms persist, please consult a doctor.

persistent.d: Obstinately refusing to give up or let go.
persistent.e: She has had a persistent cough for weeks.

personnel.e: The company hired additional personnel for the new project.

petition.d: A formal, written request made to an official person or organized body, often containing many signatures.

philosopher.d: A scholar or expert engaged in or contributing to philosophical inquiry.
philosopher.e: Chanakya was an ancient Indian philosopher and strategist.

philosophical.e: They had a deep philosophical discussion about the meaning of life.

physician.d: A medical doctor trained in human medicine.
physician.e: The physician recommended a change in diet.

pioneer.d: To be the first to do or achieve (something), preparing the way for others to follow.
pioneer.e: The young doctor pioneered a new life-saving surgical technique.

pipeline.d: A conduit made of pipes used to convey water, gas or petroleum etc.
pipeline.e: An oil pipeline has been opened from the Caspian Sea.

pirate.d: A criminal who plunders at sea; commonly attacking merchant vessels, though often pillaging port towns.
pirate.e: You should be cautious due to the Somali pirates.

pit.e: The meadow around the town is full of old pits.

plea.d: An appeal, petition, urgent prayer or entreaty.

plead.d: To present (an argument or a plea), especially in a legal case.
plead.e: The defendant has decided to plead not guilty.

pledge.d: A person who has taken a pledge of allegiance to a college fraternity, but is not yet formally approved.
pledge.e: The students took a pledge to protect the environment.

plug.d: To stop with a plug; to make tight by stopping a hole.
plug.e: He attempted to plug the leaks with some caulk.

plunge.d: To cast, stab or throw into some thing, state, condition or action.
plunge.e: to plunge a dagger into the breast;   to plunge a nation into war

pole.d: Originally, a stick; now specifically, a long and slender piece of metal or (especially) wood, used for various construction or support purposes.
pole.e: The function f(z) = \frac{1}{z-3} has a single pole at z = 3

poll.d: A polling place (usually as plural, polling places)

pond.d: The Atlantic Ocean. Especially in across the pond.
pond.e: I haven't been back home across the pond in twenty years.

portfolio.e: I would like to introduce you to our portfolio of services.

portray.d: To paint or draw the likeness of.
portray.e: I will portray a king on horseback.

post-war.d: Pertaining to a period of time immediately following the end of a war; where there is a cessation of conflict.
post-war.e: The post-war reconstruction transformed the country.

postpone.d: To delay or put off an event, appointment etc.
postpone.e: They decided to postpone the event due to heavy rain.

practitioner.d: A person who practices a profession or art, especially law or medicine.
practitioner.e: She is a medical practitioner at the local health centre.

preach.d: To teach or instruct by preaching; to inform by preaching.
preach.e: A learned local Muslim used to preach in the small mosque every Friday.

precedent.d: An act in the past which may be used as an example to help decide the outcome of similar instances in the future.

precision.d: The state of being precise or exact; exactness.

predator.d: Any animal or other organism that hunts and kills other organisms (their prey), primarily for food.
predator.e: The tiger is a top predator in the Indian jungle.

predecessor.d: A model or type of machinery or device which precedes the current one. Usually used to describe an earlier, outdated model.
predecessor.e: The new principal continued the work of her predecessor.

predominantly.d: In a predominant manner. Most commonly or frequently by a large margin.
predominantly.e: The membership is predominantly elderly, 90% are over age 60.

pregnancy.e: Regular check-ups are important during pregnancy.

prejudice.d: An irrational hostile attitude, fear or hatred towards a particular group, race or religion.
prejudice.e: I am free of all prejudices. I hate everyone equally.

preliminary.d: In preparation for the main matter; initial, introductory, preparatory.
preliminary.e: These are just the preliminary results.

premier.d: (Westminster system) The head of government in parliament and leader of the cabinet.

premise.d: A proposition antecedently supposed or proved; something previously stated or assumed as the basis of further argument; a condition; a supposition.

prescribe.d: To order (a drug or medical device) for use by a particular patient (under licensed authority).

prescription.d: The prescription medicine or intervention so prescribed.
prescription.e: The pharmacist gave her a bottle containing her prescription.

presently.e: He is presently working on a new research paper.

preservation.d: The act of preserving; care to preserve; act of keeping from destruction, decay or any ill.
preservation.e: The preservation of historical monuments is important.

preside.d: To act as president or chairperson.
preside.e: The chief justice will preside over the bench.

presidency.d: The office or role of president.
presidency.e: After many years as a party leader, she finally ascended to the presidency.

presidential.d: Pertaining to a president or presidency.
presidential.e: The presidential address was broadcast live on national television.

prestigious.e: She has a prestigious job with an international organization.

presumably.e: Presumably, he will attend the opening.

presume.d: With infinitive object: to be so presumptuous as (to do something) without proper authority or permission.
presume.e: I wouldn't presume to tell him how to do his job.

prevail.d: To succeed in persuading or inducing.
prevail.e: I prevailed on him to wait.

prevalence.d: The quality or condition of being prevalent; wide extension or spread.
prevalence.e: The prevalence of diabetes is increasing in urban India.

prevention.d: The act of preventing or hindering; obstruction of action, access, or approach; thwarting.

prey.d: A living thing that is eaten by another living thing.
prey.e: The rabbit was eaten by the coyote, so the rabbit is the coyote's prey.

privatization.d: The transfer of a company or organization from government to private ownership and control.
privatization.e: The privatization of certain services sparked a national debate.

privilege.d: (ecclesiastical law) An exemption from certain laws granted by the Pope.
privilege.e: All first-year professors here must teach four courses a term, yet you're only teaching one! What entitled you to such a privilege?

probe.e: If you probe further, you may discover different reasons.

problematic.d: Only affirming the possibility that a predicate be actualised.
problematic.e: The road conditions in rural areas can be problematic during monsoon.

proceedings.d: A measure or step taken in a course of business; a transaction
proceedings.e: a cautious or a violent proceeding

proceeds.e: They will donate all proceeds—the entire amount collected in ticket sales—from the show to charity.

processing.d: The action of the verb to process.

processor.d: A device which processes, which changes something (a computer processor, food processor, etc.).
processor.e: The food processor shredded the vegetables.

proclaim.e: The poet was proclaimed a national treasure.

productive.d: A type of set of natural numbers, related to mathematical logic.

productivity.d: The rate at which goods or services are produced by a standard population of workers
productivity.e: You can see the computer age everywhere but in the productivity statistics.

profitable.e: The startup became profitable within two years.

profound.d: Intellectually deep; entering far into subjects; reaching to the bottom of a matter, or of a branch of learning; thorough

projection.d: Something which projects, protrudes, juts out, sticks out, or stands out.
projection.e: The face of the cliff had many projections that were big enough for birds to nest on.

prominent.d: Standing out, or projecting; jutting; protuberant
prominent.e: The lawyer is a prominent figure in the legal community.

propaganda.d: A concerted set of messages aimed at influencing the opinions or behavior of large numbers of people.
propaganda.e: The students learned about propaganda techniques in history class.

proposition.d: A statement of religious doctrine; an article of faith; creed.
proposition.e: the propositions of Wyclif and Huss

prosecute.e: to prosecute a man for trespass, or for a riot

prosecution.d: The act of prosecuting a scheme or endeavor.
prosecution.e: The prosecution of the war fell to Winston Churchill.

prosecutor.d: A person, as a complainant, victim, or chief witness, who institutes prosecution in a criminal proceeding.
prosecutor.e: The prosecutor got the witness to admit he was lying.

prospective.d: Likely or expected to happen or become.
prospective.e: Prospective students are those who have already applied to the university, but have yet to be admitted.

prosperity.d: The condition of being prosperous, of having good fortune
prosperity.e: Education is the key to prosperity and progress.

protective.d: Serving, intended or wishing to protect
protective.e: Mother bears are famously protective of their cubs.

protocol.d: The minutes, or official record, of a negotiation or transaction; especially a document drawn up officially which forms the legal basis for subsequent agreements based on it.

province.d: An administrative subdivision of certain countries, including Canada and China.
province.e: Each province has its own distinct culture and traditions.

provincial.d: Of or pertaining to a province.

provision.d: A liability or contra account to recognise likely future adverse events associated with current transactions.
provision.e: We increased our provision for bad debts on credit sales going into the recession.

provoke.d: To cause someone to become annoyed or angry.
provoke.e: Don't provoke the dog; it may try to bite you.

psychiatric.e: The psychiatric ward provides care for patients with mental health issues.

pulse.d: A normally regular beat felt when arteries are depressed, caused by the pumping action of the heart.

pump.d: To use a pump to move (liquid or gas).
pump.e: I've pumped over 1000 gallons of water in the last ten minutes.

punch.e: If she punches me, I'm gonna break her nose.

query.d: A question, an inquiry (US), an enquiry (UK).
query.e: The teacher answered the student’s query concerning biosynthesis.

quest.d: A journey or effort in pursuit of a goal (often lengthy, ambitious, or fervent); a mission.
quest.e: to rove in quest of game, of a lost child, of property, etc.

quota.d: A prescribed number or percentage that may serve as, for example, a maximum, a minimum, or a goal.
quota.e: The university has a reserved quota for economically weaker students.

radar.d: A method of detecting distant objects and determining their position, velocity, or other characteristics by analysis of sent radio waves (usually microwaves) reflected from their surfaces
radar.e: His sensitive radar for hidden alliances keeps him out of trouble.

radical.d: Favoring fundamental change, or change at the root cause of a matter.

rage.e: Miniskirts were all the rage back then.

raid.d: To engage in a raid against.
raid.e: The police raided the gambling den.

rally.d: A public gathering or mass meeting that is not mainly a protest and is organized to inspire enthusiasm for a cause.

ranking.d: One’s relative placement in a list.

ratio.d: A number representing a comparison between two named things.
ratio.e: The schoolchildren ganged up to ratio the school catering over its shamefully undemocratic inclusion of vegetables in meals.

ray.d: A beam of light or radiation.
ray.e: I saw a ray of light through the clouds.

readily.d: Without unwillingness or hesitation; showing readiness.
readily.e: The suspect readily answered all questions the police officers asked him.

realization.d: The result of an artistic effort.
realization.e: The painter's realization of her concept was breathtaking.

realm.d: An abstract sphere of influence, real or imagined.
realm.e: She is an expert in the realm of international law.

rear.e: sit in the rear seats of a car

reasoning.d: The deduction of inferences or interpretations from premises; abstract thought; ratiocination.

reassure.d: To assure anew; to restore confidence to; to free from fear or self-doubt.
reassure.e: The boy's mother reassured him that there was no monster hiding under the bed.

rebel.d: A person who resists an established authority, often violently

rebellion.d: Armed resistance to an established government or ruler.
rebellion.e: The government is doing its best to stop rebellion in the country.

recipient.d: An individual receiving donor organs or tissues.
recipient.e: My e-mail never reached the intended recipient.

reconstruction.d: The act of restoring something to an earlier state.
reconstruction.e: The reconstruction of the medieval bridge began last year.

referendum.d: An action, choice, etc., which is perceived as passing judgment on another matter.
referendum.e: My father is taking my decision on whether to go to university as a referendum on his performance as a parent, and it's very stressful.

reflection.d: Something, such as an image, that is reflected.
reflection.e: The dog barked at his own reflection in the mirror.

reform.d: To form again or in a new configuration.
reform.e: The regiment reformed after surviving the first attack.

refuge.d: A state of safety, protection or shelter.

refusal.d: Depth or point at which well or borehole drilling cannot continue.
refusal.e: Her refusal to compromise earned her respect.

regain.d: To get back; to recover possession of.
regain.e: The athlete trained hard to regain his fitness after the injury.

regardless.d: Without attention to warnings or indications of bad consequences.
regardless.e: Tatiana knew the meat had passed its expiry date, but she ate it regardless.

regime.e: Heaven will eliminate the tyrannical regimes.

regulator.d: A device that controls or limits something.
regulator.e: The voltage regulator stopped working and the resulting overload destroyed the device.

regulatory.e: The regulatory body monitors food safety standards.

rehabilitation.d: The process of rehabilitating somebody or something.
rehabilitation.e: The rehabilitation centre helps patients recover from addiction.

reign.d: To exercise sovereign power, to rule as a monarch.
reign.e: He reigned in an autocratic manner.

rejection.e: He handled the rejection with grace and dignity.

relevance.d: The property or state of being relevant or pertinent.
relevance.e: The relevance of the topic to current events made it interesting.

reliability.d: The quality of being reliable, dependable or trustworthy.
reliability.e: The reliability of the bus service has improved recently.

reluctant.d: Not wanting to take some action; unwilling.
reluctant.e: She was reluctant to lend him the money

remainder.d: A part or parts remaining after some has/have been removed.
remainder.e: My son ate part of his cake and I ate the remainder.

remains.d: (chiefly in the plural) That which is left; relic; remainder.
remains.e: The victim's remains were one small piece of bone.

reminder.e: He left a note as a reminder to get groceries.

removal.d: The process of moving, or the fact of being removed.
removal.e: The removal of the old furniture made room for the new bookshelf.

render.d: To interpret, give an interpretation or rendition of.
render.e: The pianist rendered the Beethoven sonata beautifully.

renew.d: To extend a period of loan, especially a library book that is due to be returned.
renew.e: I'd like to renew these three books.  Did you know that you can renew online?

renowned.e: The movie “Three Stars” is about world-renowned chefs.

rental.d: The payment made to rent something.
rental.e: It's just a rental, so I don't need to take very good care of it.

replacement.e: The replacement of that broken light-bulb will have to wait until I can buy a new one.

reportedly.d: According to reports or rumors; supposedly.
reportedly.e: The temple is reportedly over a thousand years old.

representation.d: The ability to elect a representative to speak on one's behalf in government; the role of this representative in government.
representation.e: The lack of representation in the British parliament was one of the main factors behind the American Revolution.

reproduce.d: To produce an image or copy of.
reproduce.e: The artist can reproduce the painting in exact detail.

reproduction.e: Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.

republic.d: One of the subdivisions constituting Russia. See oblast.
republic.e: The Republic of Udmurtia is west of the Permian Oblast.

resemble.d: To be like or similar to (something); to represent as similar.

reside.d: To dwell permanently or for a considerable time; to have a settled abode for a time; to remain for a long time.
reside.e: Many IT professionals reside in Bengaluru.

residence.d: A building used as a home.
residence.e: The Prime Minister's official residence is in New Delhi.

residential.d: Of or pertaining to a place of personal residence or to a location for such places.
residential.e: They live in a residential neighborhood.

residue.d: Whatever remains after something else has been removed.
residue.e: Wash the vegetables thoroughly to remove pesticide residue.

resignation.d: A written or oral declaration that one resigns.
resignation.e: She submitted her resignation after ten years of service.

resistance.d: The act of resisting, or the capacity to resist.
resistance.e: the resistance of bacteria to certain antibiotics

respective.d: Relating to particular persons or things, each to each; particular; own.
respective.e: They returned to their respective places of abode.

respectively.d: In a relative manner; often used when comparing lists, where the term denotes that the items in the lists correspond to each other in the order they are given.
respectively.e: Serena Williams and Roger Federer won the women's and men's singles titles, respectively, at the 2010 Australian Open.

restoration.d: The return of a former monarchy or monarch to power, usually after having been forced to step down.
restoration.e: The restoration of the House of Stuart took place a few years after the death of Cromwell.

restraint.d: Something that restrains, ties, fastens or secures
restraint.e: Make sure all the restraints are tight.

resume.d: To start (something) again that has been stopped or paused from the point at which it was stopped or paused; continue, carry on.
resume.e: We will resume this discussion tomorrow at nine.

retreat.d: To withdraw from a position, go back.

retrieve.d: To regain or get back something.
retrieve.e: to retrieve one's character or independence; to retrieve a thrown ball

revelation.d: The act of revealing or disclosing.
revelation.e: The revelation of the new product excited the audience.

revenge.d: Any form of personal retaliatory action against an individual, institution, or group for some alleged or perceived harm or injustice.

reverse.d: To turn something around so that it faces the opposite direction or runs in the opposite sequence.
reverse.e: to reverse a portion of video footage

revival.d: Renewed prevalence of something, as a practice or a fashion.

revive.d: To recover from a state of oblivion, obscurity, neglect, or depression.
revive.e: Classical learning revived in the fifteenth century.

revolutionary.d: Of or pertaining to a revolution in government; tending to, or promoting, revolution

rhetoric.d: The art of using language, especially public speaking, as a means to persuade.

rifle.d: A shouldered firearm with a long, rifled barrel to improve range and accuracy.

riot.d: Wanton or unrestrained behavior; uproar; tumult.
riot.e: In summer this flower garden is a riot of colour.

rip.d: To tear apart; to rapidly become two parts.
rip.e: My shirt ripped when it was caught on a bramble.

ritual.d: Rite; a repeated set of actions
ritual.e: They carried out the ritual carefully.

robust.e: A robust wall was put up.

rock.e: Pearl Rock near Cape Cod is so named because the morning sun makes it gleam like a pearl.

rod.d: A unit of area equal to a square rod, 30 1/4 square yards or 1/160 acre.
rod.e: The house had a small yard of about six rods in size.

rotate.e: He rotated in his chair to face me.

rotation.d: The act of turning around a centre or an axis.
rotation.e: The earth's rotation about its axis is responsible for its being slightly oblate rather than a sphere.

ruling.d: An order or a decision on a point of law from someone in authority.

rumour.d: A statement or claim of questionable accuracy, from no known reliable source, usually spread by word of mouth.
rumour.e: There's a rumor going round that he's going to get married.

sack.d: To put in a sack or sacks.

sacred.d: Characterized by solemn religious ceremony or religious use, especially, in a positive sense; consecrated, made holy.

sacrifice.d: To offer (something) as a gift to a deity.
sacrifice.e: Venison has many advantages over meat from factory farms, although it still requires a hunter to sacrifice the life of a deer.

saint.d: A person whom a church or another religious group has officially recognised as especially holy or godly; one eminent for piety and virtue.
saint.e: Kateri Tekakwitha was proclaimed a saint.

sake.d: The benefit or regard of someone or something

sanction.d: An approval, by an authority, generally one that makes something valid.
sanction.e: The whalers had been operating in the contested waters off the island with sanction from the Japanese government.

sceptical.e: I can see why people are so skeptical [sic] about him, but I think he's on to something here. (regarded by organizations such as the BBC as an error)

scope.d: The breadth, depth or reach of a subject; a domain.

screw.d: To connect or assemble pieces using a screw.
screw.e: the skeleton screw (Caprella);  the sand screw

scrutiny.d: Intense study of someone or something.
scrutiny.e: The government project came under public scrutiny.

seal.d: To place in a sealed container.
seal.e: I've sealed the documents in this envelope.

secular.d: Not bound by the vows of a monastic order.

seemingly.d: In a seemly manner; decorously; with propriety.
seemingly.e: The seemingly simple puzzle turned out to be very difficult.

seize.d: To bind or lock in position immovably; see also seize up.
seize.e: Rust caused the engine to seize, never to run again.

selective.d: Of or relating to natural selection.

senator.d: A member, normally elected, in the house or chamber of a legislature called a senate. The legislatures of the United States and Canada have senators.
senator.e: The senator spoke passionately about education reform.

sensation.d: A widespread reaction of interest or excitement.
sensation.e: The cool breeze was a pleasant sensation on the hot day.

sensitivity.d: The ability of an organism or organ to respond to external stimuli.
sensitivity.e: The teacher handled the issue with great sensitivity.

sentiment.d: A general thought, feeling, or sense.
sentiment.e: The sentiment emerged that we were acting too soon.

separation.d: The act of disuniting two or more things, or the condition of being separated.
separation.e: The separation of duties ensures fairness in the organisation.

serial.d: Having to do with or arranged in a series.
serial.e: He was a serial entrepreneur, always coming up with a new way to make cash.

set-up.d: The fashion in which something is organized or arranged.
set-up.e: The classroom setup was simple and efficient.

settlement.e: The legal settlement was reached out of court.

severely.e: The crops were severely damaged by the hailstorm.

sexuality.d: A person's identity in relation to the gender they are attracted to.
sexuality.e: Everyone should be respected regardless of their sexuality.

sexy.e: The advertisement featured a sexy new car design.

shaped.d: Having been given a shape, especially a curved shape.
shaped.e: The shaped sides of the wardrobe give it a more attractive appearance.

shareholder.d: One who owns shares of stock in a corporation.
shareholder.e: Shareholders are the real owners of a publicly traded business, but management runs it.

shatter.d: To violently break something into pieces.
shatter.e: The miners used dynamite to shatter rocks.

shed.e: A metal comb shed her golden hair.

sheer.e: Her light, sheer dress caught everyone’s attention.

shipping.e: The shipping' is included in the quoted price.

shore.d: Land adjoining a non-flowing body of water, such as an ocean, lake or pond.
shore.e: lake shore;  bay shore;  gulf shore;  island shore;  mainland shore;  river shore;  estuary shore;  pond shore;  sandy shore;  rocky shore

short-term.d: Of or pertaining to the near or immediate future.
short-term.e: This short-term plan deals with the next few days.

shortage.d: A lack or deficiency; an insufficient amount.
shortage.e: The water shortage affected many villages during the summer.

shortly.e: Ideas are generally expressed more shortly in verse than in prose

shrug.d: To raise (the shoulders) to express uncertainty, lack of concern, (formerly) dread, etc.
shrug.e: I asked him for an answer and he just shrugged.

sibling.d: A person who shares a parent; one's brother or sister who one shares a parent with.
sibling.e: None of my siblings are married yet.

sigh.d: To experience an emotion associated with sighing.
sigh.e: He silently sighed for his lost youth.

signature.d: A person's name, written by that person, used as identification or to signify approval of accompanying material, such as a legal contract.
signature.e: Your signature must not exceed 600 pixels.

significance.d: The extent to which something matters; importance
significance.e: As a juror your opinion is of great significance for the outcome of the trial.

simulate.d: To model, replicate, duplicate the behavior, appearance or properties of.
simulate.e: We will use a smoke machine to simulate the fog you will actually encounter.

simulation.d: Something that simulates a system or environment in order to predict actual behaviour.
simulation.e: The most reliable simulation predicts that the hurricane will turn north.

simultaneously.e: The event was broadcast simultaneously on television and radio.

sin.d: A violation of God's will or religious law.
sin.e: As a Christian, I think this is a sin against God.

sketch.d: A rapidly executed freehand drawing that is not intended as a finished work, often consisting of a multitude of overlapping lines.

skip.d: To move by hopping on alternate feet.
skip.e: She will skip from one end of the sidewalk to the other.

skull.d: The main bones of the head considered as a unit; including the cranium, facial bones, and mandible.
skull.e: The divers encountered a huge school of mackerel.

slam.d: To shut with sudden force so as to produce a shock and noise.

slap.e: She slapped him in response to the insult.

slash.d: To cut or attempt to cut, particularly:
slash.e: The province's traditional slash-and-burn agriculture was only sustainable with a much smaller population.

slavery.d: An institution or social practice of owning human beings as property, especially for use as forced laborers.
slavery.e: The museum exhibit highlighted the history of slavery.

slogan.d: A catch phrase associated with the product or service being advertised.
slogan.e: The campaign slogan inspired millions of people.

slot.d: A slot machine designed for gambling.
slot.e: I walked past the poker tables and went straight to the slots.

smash.d: To be destroyed by being smashed.
smash.e: The crockery smashed as it hit the floor.

snap.d: To cause something to emit a snapping sound.

so-called.d: So named; commonly called by such a name, often with a connotation that the item is not worthy of that name.
so-called.e: These so-called "assistants" are making our jobs harder, not easier!

soak.d: To be saturated with liquid by being immersed in it.
soak.e: I'm going to soak in the bath for a couple of hours.

soar.d: To rise, especially rapidly or unusually high.
soar.e: The pump prices soared into new heights as the strike continued.

socialist.d: Of, promoting, practicing, or characteristic of socialism.
socialist.e: The party follows socialist principles of equal distribution.

solely.e: The new chef was solely responsible for attending the grill.

solicitor.d: In parts of the U.S., the chief legal officer of a city, town or other jurisdiction.
solicitor.e: The solicitor advised his client on the legal matter.

solidarity.d: Willingness to give psychological and/or material support when another person is in a difficult position or needs affection.
solidarity.e: Only the solidarity provided by her siblings allowed Margaret to cope with her mother's harrowing death.

solo.d: Of, or relating to, a musical solo.
solo.e: She gave a solo performance at the music festival.

somehow.d: In one way or another; in a way not yet known or explained; by some means
somehow.e: This problem has to be tackled somehow.

sometime.d: At an indefinite but stated time in the past or future.
sometime.e: I'll see you at the pub sometime this evening.

sophisticated.d: Having obtained worldly experience, and lacking naiveté; cosmopolitan.

sovereignty.d: (of a polity) The state of making laws and controlling resources without the coercion of other nations.
sovereignty.e: Every nation has the right to protect its sovereignty.

spam.e: I get far too much spam.

span.d: To measure by the span of the hand with the fingers extended, or with the fingers encompassing the object.
span.e: to span a space or distance; to span a cylinder

spare.d: Held in reserve, to be used in an emergency.
spare.e: a spare anchor; a spare bed or room

spark.d: To give off a spark or sparks.
spark.e: The speech sparked a lively debate among the students.

specialize.d: To make distinct or separate, particularly:
specialize.e: The hospital specializes in cardiac surgery.

specialized.d: Highly skilled in a specific field.

specification.d: An explicit set of requirements to be satisfied by a material, product, or service.
specification.e: The building was constructed according to strict specifications.

specify.d: To state explicitly, or in detail, or as a condition.
specify.e: Please specify the size and colour of the shirt you want.

specimen.d: An individual instance that represents a class; an example.
specimen.e: early specimens of the art of Picasso

spectacle.d: An exciting or extraordinary scene, exhibition, performance etc.
spectacle.e: The horse race was a thrilling spectacle.

spectacular.d: Amazing or worthy of special notice.

spectator.d: One who watches an event; especially, one held outdoors.
spectator.e: The cheering spectators watched the fireworks.

spectrum.d: Specifically, a range of colours representing light (electromagnetic radiation) of contiguous frequencies; hence electromagnetic spectrum, visible spectrum, ultraviolet spectrum, etc.
spectrum.e: The course covers a wide spectrum of topics.

speculate.d: To anticipate which branch of code will be chosen and execute it in advance.
speculate.e: Analysts speculate that the economy will grow by seven per cent.

speculation.d: A conclusion to which the mind comes by speculating; mere theory; notion; conjecture.
speculation.e: There is much speculation about the new government policy.

sphere.d: A regular three-dimensional object in which every cross-section is a circle; the figure described by the revolution of a circle about its diameter .

spice.d: Aromatic or pungent plant matter (usually dried) used to season or flavour food.

spin.d: To make yarn by twisting and winding fibers together.
spin.e: They spin the cotton into thread.

spine.d: Something resembling a backbone, such as a ridge, or a long, central structure from which other structures radiate.
spine.e: The doctor examined her spine for any problems.

spotlight.d: A bright, directional light or lamp, especially one used to illuminate the focus or center of attention on a stage.

spouse.d: A person in a marriage or marital relationship.
spouse.e: People should treat their spouses with respect.

spy.e: During the Cold War, Russia and America would each spy on each other for recon.

squad.d: A group of people organized for some common purpose, usually of about ten members.

squeeze.d: To apply pressure to from two or more sides at once.
squeeze.e: I squeezed the ball between my hands.

stab.d: To thrust in a stabbing motion.
stab.e: to stab a dagger into a person

stability.d: The condition of being stable or in equilibrium, and thus resistant to change.
stability.e: Political stability is important for economic growth.

stabilize.e: Jody stabilized the table by putting a book under the short leg.

stake.d: A piece of wood or other material, usually long and slender, pointed at one end so as to be easily driven into the ground as a marker or a support or stay.
stake.e: We have surveyor's stakes at all four corners of this field, to mark exactly its borders.

stark.e: I picked my way forlornly through the stark, sharp rocks.

statistical.e: The report is based on statistical data from the census.

steer.d: To guide the course of a vessel, vehicle, aircraft etc. (by means of a device such as a rudder, paddle, or steering wheel).

stem.e: to stem cherries; to stem tobacco leaves

stereotype.d: A conventional, formulaic, and often oversimplified or exaggerated conception, opinion, or image of (a person).

stimulus.d: Any external phenomenon that has an influence on a system, by triggering or modifying an internal phenomenon.

stir.e: She stirred the pudding with a spoon.

storage.d: The act of storing goods; the state of being stored.
storage.e: There's a lot of storage space in the loft.

straightforward.d: Proceeding in a straight course or manner; not deviating.
straightforward.e: The instructions were straightforward and easy to follow.

strain.d: The blood-vessel in the yolk of an egg.
strain.e: There is a strain of madness in her family.

strand.d: The shore or beach of the sea or ocean; shore; beach.

strategic.e: The fort was built in a strategic location on the hilltop.

striking.e: He looked quite striking in his new suit and tie.

structural.d: Of, relating to, or having structure.

stumble.d: To make a mistake or have trouble.
stumble.e: I always stumble over verbs in Spanish.

stun.d: To incapacitate; especially by inducing disorientation or unconsciousness.
stun.e: Bill tried to stun the snake by striking it on the head.

submission.d: A submission hold in wrestling, mixed martial arts, or other combat sports.
submission.e: Any submissions received after Friday will have marks deducted for lateness.

subscriber.d: A person who subscribes to a publication or a service
subscriber.e: Our magazine has over ten thousand subscribers.

subscription.d: Access to a resource for a period of time, generally for payment.
subscription.e: I have a monthly subscription to the Daily Telegraph newspaper.

subsidy.d: Financial support or assistance, such as a grant.
subsidy.e: Manufacturing firms are supported by government subsidies in some countries.

substantially.d: In a strong substantial manner; considerably.
substantially.e: The population of the city has grown substantially over the decade.

substitute.d: To use in place of something else, with the same function.
substitute.e: I had no shallots so I substituted onion.

substitution.d: The act of substituting or the state of being substituted.
substitution.e: The coach made a substitution in the second half of the match.

subtle.d: Hard to grasp; not obvious or easily understood; barely noticeable.
subtle.e: The difference is subtle, but you can hear it if you listen carefully.

suburban.d: Relating to or characteristic of or situated on the outskirts of a city.
suburban.e: They moved to a suburban neighbourhood for a quieter life.

succession.d: A sequence of things in order.
succession.e: Three batsmen were dismissed in quick succession.

successive.d: Coming one after the other in a series.
successive.e: They had won the title for five successive years.

successor.d: A person or thing that immediately follows another in holding an office or title.
successor.e: George W. Bush was successor to Bill Clinton as President of the US.

suck.d: To use the mouth and lips to pull in (a liquid, especially milk from the breast).
suck.e: The baby sucked milk from the bottle.

sue.d: To leave high and dry on shore.

suite.d: A retinue or company of attendants, as of a distinguished personage

summit.d: A gathering or assembly of leaders.
summit.e: They met for an international summit on environmental issues.

superb.d: First-rate; of the highest quality; exceptionally good.

superior.d: More comprehensive, as a term in classification.
superior.e: A genus is superior to a species.

supervise.d: To oversee or direct a task or organization.
supervise.e: Without someone to supervise them, the group will lack direction.

supervision.d: The act or instance of supervising.
supervision.e: Under his parents' supervision he drilled the holes in the wood.

supervisor.d: In certain states, an elected member of the governing body for a county which is called the board of supervisors.
supervisor.e: The supervisor ensured all safety rules were followed on site.

supplement.d: To provide or make a supplement to something.
supplement.e: There is a £2 supplement if you choose the steak.

supportive.e: I had a very supportive coach who helped me.

supposedly.d: As a matter of supposition; in the beliefs or according to the claims of some people.
supposedly.e: According to your testimony, you were supposedly at home watching TV when the murder occurred.

suppress.d: To restrain or repress, such as laughter or an expression.
suppress.e: I struggled to suppress my smile.

supreme.d: Dominant, having power over all others.

surge.d: To rush, flood, or increase suddenly.

surgical.d: Of, relating to, used in, or resulting from surgery.
surgical.e: The building was destroyed with a surgical air-strike.

surplus.d: That which remains when use or need is satisfied, or when a limit is reached; excess; overplus.

surrender.d: To yield (oneself) to an influence, emotion, passion, etc.
surrender.e: to surrender oneself to grief, to despair, to indolence, or to sleep

surveillance.d: Continuous monitoring of disease occurrence for example.
surveillance.e: CCTV surveillance cameras have been installed at all entry points.

suspension.d: A stay or postponement of the execution of a sentence, usually by letters of suspension granted on application to the Lord Ordinary.
suspension.e: suspension from school as a disciplinary measure

suspicious.e: I have a suspicious attitude to get-rich-quick schemes.

sustain.d: To maintain, or keep in existence.
sustain.e: The city came under sustained attack by enemy forces.

swing.d: To rotate about an off-centre fixed point.

I noticed in this bus a taxpayer with a long taxpayer's neck and whose taxpayer's head bore a taxpayer's felt hat encircled by a plait the like of which no taxpayer ever wore before. Suddenly the said taxpayer peremptorily addressed a nearby taxpayer, complaining bitterly that he was purposely treading on his taxpayer's toes every time other taxpayers got on or off the taxpayers' bus. Then the angry taxpayer went and sat down in a seat for taxpayers which another taxpayer had just vacated. — Raymond Queneau, 'from Polyptotes' in Exercises in Style, 1945 (Eng. 1958)
